speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with William Stowell, who is known by most people as Michigan Bill, took place at his home in Lewiston on October 29, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager. There is some technical difficulty on this tape. I come from Canada, but I worked all over up in the Upper Peninsula. I was born in Canada. I've heard that Upper Peninsula there in Michigan was some logging country. Yeah, it was. It's all cut out. It's been three years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you wind up in Michigan? In Michigan? Yeah, how did you wind up there?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, just cramping around. I dropped a job, you know. Finally wound up there. But after a few years there, not too long. Maybe six, seven years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When?

speaker: William Stowell
words: In the early 20s. 20, 21, 22.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I've heard something about that country. There were a lot of Finns in there. Oh, Christ, yes. That Oak and Hancock Calumet, that was all Finns. That mining country, that was copper. Yeah, that was 90 % Finns. Yeah, I knew some of them. Not all. I didn't ever work on mines, you know.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I was around with them a lot. They worked the woods, too, of course. Yeah, a lot of Finns there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you come out here from there?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this country very much like that as far as what the logging life was like?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, practically the same when I came here except with steeper ground, you know. Theirs was all level. But here's pretty hilly, you know, steep. Different little, different logging, you know. Of course they used horses both places then, but they used dogs in this country, you know. Nailed together, you see. And trailed them, you know, long trails. Well, 20, 25 at the most, you know. And then shoots. And flumes. They'd shoot them to the flume and then flume them to the river. That's the way they done it here. It was too steep for railroads. There weren't no trucks at that time, so that's the way they did it. You know, they shoot. Well, that's the way it went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the same thing for like the lockers? Same kind of guys out here as there was back there?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, there was more foreigners back there than here. But otherwise it was the same. Camps, you know, conditions are different here. They were better, much better than they were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they better here?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, Lord, yes. Yeah. They, well, they was all, they had eliminated the top bunks. Well, that was later years they done that, but there was double deck bunks there, you know, two in a bunk, and up and lower, and lousy. Everything else is bad business. That's one reason I got out of there. Not much chance to improve it. But this country here was way different. We got clean linen, you know, every week for your bed. It got to be later years old, bottom bunks, you know, there was no top. Oh, yeah, it's much better here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear much about the IWWs, the work they did to improve conditions?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, yes, they accomplished wonders.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. They did. It was kind of a rough and tough outfit, they, but they got the work done, they got the job done. Direct action was what it was. There was no political harem-scarum mixed up in it. It was all direct, on the job. That's how it worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was it that you got here?

speaker: William Stowell
words: In Idaho. 27. Well, by that time now, the way I've heard it is it was back in about 1917. Oh, yes, yes, yes. The IWWs had cleaned up the camps. Well, up until 23, the last big strike was in 23, a general strike, for 10 days. It was just, well, it was for the prisoners that went in jail, you see. Those that were put in jail during these strikes, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean during the earlier ones?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was to get them out?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, no. They didn't make it, but they shut everything down for 10 days. But there, that's all it was.

speaker: William Stowell
words: They couldn't get it. But they did make a little uprising here in 36th again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember that, don't you?

speaker: William Stowell
words: I've heard about that. Yeah. That was for conditions, mostly, and wages. Well, they wanted a minimum of $5 a day. And clean up the camps. The camps were getting pretty scroungy, you know.

speaker: William Stowell
words: And I don't know whether it was won or lost. We were out a couple of months. I was working for the putt last then, but we were out a couple of months. I don't believe we lost anything. They cleaned the camps up and raised the wages. But they didn't do that until after the strike, you see. They broke the strike, that's what happened. Well, you know, that's the story that I've heard before. That way the company says that it wasn't the IWWs who made the changes because they tried to end the strike and then they make the changes. Yeah. But it's all one and the same thing. Yeah. That's still why you look at it that way. They didn't do it until after the strike, but they did it.

speaker: William Stowell
words: But that was the last feeble attempt that the IWW ever made in this country. I was surprised when I heard about the 36 strike that they were still kicking around all that time because these others, what, the AF of L and those groups were getting in and trying to, and the CIO too, doing some more than that. Well, the CIO wasn't too husky yet in this country, you know. They come in afterwards. The AF of L got booted out and the CIO took over by a large margin, you know. But, well, I don't know, the AF of L worn out, I guess. They were, well, the CIO was political too, but the other was more so, you know. I never belonged, I belonged to the CIO, but I never did belong to the AF of L in the woods, you know. I belonged to the pilot, I was the like of that. Well, I had the idea that the AF of L was more like a craft union than it was a real industrial union. Sure, sure it was. Yes, it was, yes. It was a craft union, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did that make more sense to me, the CIO, would be the organization of the lumber industry?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, yeah. It was, well, that's the real of the two, but I don't know, I, means that I had belonged to the IWW, I couldn't see much dealing with them. No, I couldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think made the IWW so much a better union than the CIO?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, you could act more or less without all these executive boards and the like of that, yes. Now, you could hold a meeting in Tampa if you decided to do something, and the majority would take a vote, and if the majority wanted it, YAA, that's the way it was. It went, as far as the crew was concerned. We didn't always win, but it was an effort anyway.

speaker: William Stowell
words: So, that was what I like about it, that these other organizations have too much political aid, high salaries, big shots, all that is all. It don't work with the common stiff, you know. I don't figure it does. You know, it cost a lot of these unions that never gets down to working, and I mean, he never really has any say so about it. No decisions were made up there. Yeah, yeah, yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you ever, were you ever in camps that, where you guys took a vote and then went out on strike?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of reason would make you decide you had to do that?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, well, different reasons. Mostly, especially the IWW, they were mostly for conditions, you know. Working conditions and living conditions. Get away from them old double bunks and lousy camps and bum chuck and all that, you know, trying to get something better to, well, a better living. It's what it was, really. They never did strike too much for money, because they take that away from you anyway, you know, that money.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, I don't, oh, I think, of course, the IWWs never come back, you know. Not as the IWW. They're done.

speaker: William Stowell
words: They were revolutionary, you know. That was the core of the, of their constitution. Yeah, and a revolutionary organization, that this DNA won't, won't get along very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it made more sense in the old days than it would make nowadays?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, that's kind of a hard question to answer. I wouldn't, I wouldn't attempt to answer it, I... No, I don't know. Conditions have changed a lot. I don't know, I sometimes, when I look at history, I sometimes wonder, you know, whether it's changed all that much, or whether it's just... People have changed, you know. People have changed an awful lot. Oh, yes. That's what makes it, I think.

speaker: William Stowell
words: It's the people themselves. You see, the lumberjack was always a single man, a footloose man, you know. He didn't give a goddamn for anything, you know. He had nothing to lose, and the world to gain. So that's what made him that way, I believe. I'm pretty sure that's the way it works for me. He's a camp full of, say, 150 men in a camp. They might be two percent married, or maybe one percent, all single.

speaker: William Stowell
words: And they had no responsibility, except themselves. And they could gain with... for them. That was the... Now you see their ordinary... they got responsibilities. They had payments on cars and phones and TVs and whatnot. Them days, the older days, oh, they had to just pack that, just what was on his back, that's all. You know, it seems so different than it is when I think about it, when you're saying then and now. I can't even think of how the situation would rise, if so many men would be able to be single and not be tied down by anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's hard to figure that out, isn't it?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. I don't know why they never got married then, I can't figure that out. Well, I never did, but I was just, well, from job to job, you know, and I didn't have no responsibility, couldn't afford to have any. Unless I wanted to settle down and take less money for some other job, you know, we always made a little more than the town workers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why was that? Because you guys could go any place where there was a job?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Wherever the money was, yeah. Well, when the gyppos just come in, you know, of course that changed everything, the game. It was a minimum wage, but it was awful small. Everybody was a gyppo, gyppo, gyppo, gyppo. And they always made it so you could, well, you'd come out a third above your regular wages anyway, you see, by going the limit. You know, doing the utmost, your very best. Every day you had to, you had to.

speaker: William Stowell
words: The way that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a good deal?

speaker: William Stowell
words: I mean, it sounds to me like you must have got a lot more work out of it. Oh, yes, sure. No, that wasn't a good deal for us at all. No, we tried to abolish it, but you couldn't. I don't know, there's too many that wanted it that way. You know, really wanted it that way, and they were right down in their heart, they wanted it that way. I mean, good, militant union men, too, they weren't really what I'd call good militant men, but if there was any trouble at all, they were always ready to help out. Most of them wanted a gyppo system, you know, a little more money. You know, that's what drives people out of that money. Yeah, yeah. Sure, well, for a few years, I mean, a guy can break his back working to be young, and you know, when they really can put out their work, but it seems like it's going to catch up with them after a while. Well, it does, you know, and that's what it does, sure. You may think you can do it, but you can't. You know, you poop out pretty goddamn sudden, like when you get older. That's awful hard work. In the olden days, you know, everything was hand-work. Falling was all hand-work, bucking was all hand-work, and the skidding was all well, the horses, you know, but PB work, you know, you had all that to do, that went into work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the IW have a position on that? Did they say gyppo no good?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, I have to stand there too. That's really not good, no. It seems to me that a guy who, if you really believed in, you know, in the union, then the gyppo would be very unequal, because the guys that could put out the work would make a lot better than the old-timers, let's say, who couldn't do it. Yes, but when you got old enough, you quit gyppo, no, you couldn't. That was the way that worked.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, you were good up to 50 years old, you know, you knew a lot of tricks, a lot of shortcuts by that time. You know, you didn't know when you were younger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Just generally speaking, you know, I can't... The way to do the work, you mean like you learned, you really mastered the whole job. Yeah, sure, that's it, that's it. Yeah, that's my idea, yes.

speaker: William Stowell
words: It, well... Just as I say, you learned a lot of tricks and shortcuts, and it all helped out at the end of the day, you know, you accomplished a little more than the absolute green one would, and done it just about as easy probably.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I've heard that most of the early lumberjacks around this country were old, I've heard that most of the early lumberjacks out in this country, they were old men, or older men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true, so far as you know, in the early days?

speaker: William Stowell
words: There were a lot of old men, yes. There were a lot of them pretty old when they come to this country, you know, from the east. They were all from the east, practically. Eastern Canada. And the midwestern states, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Michigan. That's where most of them were from when I came out here, and they were all men along in their years then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were one of the young ones when you came out?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, yes, yes, sure.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I was only about 23, I think, when I landed there.

speaker: William Stowell
words: But all I ever did was work in the woods, you know, and I was a kid at home. So it was, well, a little man had a temper, you know. An old sawmill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, an old waterflower sawmill.

speaker: William Stowell
words: He must have seen it ever since you were old enough to see things. Yes, sure. Yeah. Did he let you work on it when you were... Oh no. Oh no. No, I just was around it, you know. I never, I never did nothing around it. Probably a little lumber or something like that. Worked on the ponds a little bit too. I was going to school then. I didn't do much work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that in Canada?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, it was in Quebec. The province of Quebec. A little small town, but size fierce, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any operations you had there?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh no, the Hill Harris Sawyer, that's all. The rest of it was level of neighbors. He was working for a little lumber. He had a...

speaker: William Stowell
words: Worked there too to get some lumber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you come down to the states because there was more work?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, no. I come down to go to school. Oh, huh. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were still just a kid?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, just a, yeah. I was seven, I guess.

speaker: William Stowell
words: You know, in the Midwest, lumber towns were really tough towns. Oh yeah. It was a good idea. Of course they had to calm down some when I got there. They used to fight for the fun of it, but I don't know, they kind of cut that out. People change.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I heard you. You know, it's mostly... The main fights was over drinking and that kind of thing. Well, yes, they'd be one tough guy and another guy would think he was tougher. He'd maybe go to the camp. The other guy was working there just to challenge him. That's the way that worked. And they were rough too. A lot of them done all their fighting on the floor. They were chewing and biting. If they were on their feet, they were using their shoes. Yeah, they went in to win.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the grudges went on for a very long time? Huh? Do the grudges go on for a very long time?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh no. They get forgotten. Oh no, they ever get forgotten. Sometimes they weren't, but... Most of the time they were working on it. Have it out of the mobile ways. Go back to drinking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was that the same out here too?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Was the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The town was pretty tough? Out here in the log town?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Some of them were, yes. Like Aberdeen and Oakland. Aberdeen was a rough town. Washington. That was about the roughest on this west coast, I think.

speaker: William Stowell
words: There was a long storm in there and lumberjacks and everything. Timber was close. To town. And that made a difference and there were still a single men working in it all then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were staying with the job for a very long time? Me?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. Yeah, I have stayed... 6-8 months. And I stayed 2 days. Oh, very different. I depend on how the job was or something.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I usually stay pretty good though.

speaker: William Stowell
words: It's... Well, the whole of them, I was thinking of going... As soon as you stayed, it didn't go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About the different bosses?

speaker: William Stowell
words: The different bosses that formed it. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make the big difference usually?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, it would, yeah. Sometimes.

speaker: William Stowell
words: But... With some people... With me, it just makes me remember who I worked for. I didn't work for anybody, as long as I get along with them. Of course, it never interfered with your mother. If you were a Jew, you wouldn't see them maybe from the time you started in until you quit. You'd see them in camp, you know, but you wouldn't see them on the job or nothing. But if you worked for the day, you'd see them. You'd be around then, every day. They didn't do it. But they didn't bother you much, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a difference, much difference between them?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Were some...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were some form of really working for the company and others working more for the men?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well... You can't be both ways. You couldn't be both ways, you know. You... Some leaned a little bit towards the crew. If they were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If they come up from the crew, you know what I mean?

speaker: William Stowell
words: If they were picked out of the crew, something like that, you know. They would lean a little towards... Their fellow man, but ordinarily, of course, he was working for the company. But... You can't hold that against them. You know, that was their idea. In order to hold their job, they had to be. That was my idea always.

speaker: William Stowell
words: As a general... Generally speaking, there were a pretty good bunch of guys. I never seen any of them once, anyway.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, Axel Anderson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know him?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yeah. I wouldn't be. He's living yet. In Spokane, I hear. Yeah, I've talked to him up there. I've talked to him for hours now. Christ, he must be old. About 91, maybe.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Did you know Melker, his brother?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, I didn't know him. I've heard of him. He was a good guy. A real good guy. Well, Axel was all right, too, but Melker was a real good fella. He killed himself, he shot himself. That's what I heard. He's drinking.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, he was an awful genius. He was a foreman, too, I think. Sure. A good man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think? I mean, what would make a good foreman like him? What did he do that made him good?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, I don't know. He mixed with the crew in town and on the job, and he drank with them, and just got a ton of his speak, and he got along real good with them. He gave them a good price for their work. And he got a lot of work done. That was the whole sum and something of that deal.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. Well, Axel said that he thinks a push was in a kind of mind between the company and the men. He said he used to like it, to try to do good by the men, and every once in a while he'd get on the carpet with the company. Yeah, sure, sure. I imagine they did. I know they did. I never was a push, but I can imagine that some of them would get caught on the carpet. For being a little bit too lenient, or lean a little bit towards the crew, you know. I know they do, sure they do. They're bound to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Malcolm a big man like Axel?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, he was tall, but a little taller than me. Axel, but he was slimmer.

speaker: William Stowell
words: He was a little different man than Axel. I don't know why, but he was.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Axel was pretty much the company. But Malcolm, he didn't seem good. He was not way too heavy, but he was a good push. He was good for, really good for both sides. He got a lot of work done, and he was good with the crew.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I always liked him really well. I worked for the both of them. I used to work for Melter whenever I was on that side, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After they see this outfit here, what time did they consolidate?

speaker: William Stowell
words: 31. Something like that, yeah. I remember I was working up here at Camp H, and we got our September, October checks. They had a little strip of paper up in the corner, white strip of paper. It used to be the Clearwater Timber Company, you know. The checks were made out of them, by the way. And then here was a little white strip of paper, pasted on to the check, and said, by the way, that's for us. I think that was in 31. I'm pretty sure it was. I know I was working up at Camp H for old Brooks.

speaker: William Stowell
words: That was it. Malcolm was, he was on the other side. Yeah, he was always over there. Mm-hm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much difference between the two sides?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The camps and the men? What? The camps and the men?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yes. Yes. That other side, they had always had better truck, better camp conditions and everything. That's before they consolidated, you know. It was the...

speaker: William Stowell
words: ...part of the last lumber company. Yeah. That's what it was. Oh, yes. They had awful good horses. I just like to go over there on account of the horses. They were good. They took good care of them. You couldn't abuse a team of horses there, you know, or you wouldn't stay. Them pushers were all pretty much horse lovers, you know. And that old Knoggle, though, he was horse straight, that guy. Yeah. Oh, Jesus. He'd sleep with them, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't he do most of the buying for him?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. He was a pusher. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Knoggle, you mean?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. Sure, he did. Well, him and the vet. But Knoggle was the guy that bought him. I don't know where the hell he got them then. Well, they got them for this operation. Here, you used to get to buy you horses in Oregon. I know in the early days, they bought quite a few from the farmers. Yeah. That's what I heard. Yeah, sure. That's when they bought them all.

speaker: William Stowell
words: And then there was outfits in that raised them, you know. That was their business, raising horses. They used to buy them. They bought wherever they could. This vet here on this side, White Chuck, White, he got over in class. He was 50 when he got here, I think. But I went with him a couple of times buying horses. He'd hear of a team of horses someplace, you know. He'd hear of them, you know. Hear about them. He'd go and look at them. If they weren't too old and looked good to him, well, yeah, he'd buy them. If they were well-matched, you know. But they used to buy them. Oh, they'd buy one here and one there, maybe half a dozen here, and go on and match them up themselves. After they got here. Bo Viddle had a nice big meadow over there, you know. Geez, I used to go in and look at them horses. Oh, God. So I can grasp up to their knees. Fat and sick. Yeah. Nice big barn. I used to like that. Gee, they had a lot of horses, too. Oh, Christ.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think they had, what, probably 250, 300 horses in that barn?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yes. See, I'd been in that barn. I brought horses out of there one time over here. That was in the fall of 32. I don't know how many teams we had. We must have had 30 pair anyway. We found out we were staying overnight, then we come across to Norfolk, the big island, and then the headquarters. Old Nottle hated to see them horses leave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you take them? I mean, was there many guys on the run like that?

speaker: William Stowell
words: There was about four of us, I guess, or five. Of course, I knew the route, too, but there was a guy that knew the route. That is, from Elk River to Big Island.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I don't know, maybe six. But we had a wagon with all the harness in. They were unharnessed, unharnessed from all, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just drove?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, no. Put the harness in the wagon and then led them behind in twos, you know. Had four horses on the wagon and then lead them behind in. There was a guy with a saddle horse. He took a big butch, too. That's where we got them over. Oh, they were all right after they'd be on the road a little while, you know. They kind of calmed down, but otherwise, when we first got them over the way, they were all harnessed then, you know. Had the harness on them. They were tangled up. Lose a team once in a while, had to start and pick them up again. But we made them in, I think, three days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many horses did you take?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh,

speaker: William Stowell
words: they must have been 35 pairs, anyway. Yeah, I can't remember exactly, but... Not like a pretty good job. Yeah. I don't, the full-built side wasn't running nothing then at all. And this outwork didn't run nothing that summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you mean this is when the Depression was on?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. Yeah, in 32. And they started up a couple camps here in the fall. That was that rainy fall. The bridges washed out and everything. Between RPO and headquarters. They had to quit at New Year's time. Christmas was the high water. I think there was 14 or 15 bridges or now between headquarters and RPO. On the railroad campus, I guess it is.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, I've heard it up there said that it was a lot rougher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over here?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Rougher. Up there, one or two guys said to me that they had the idea that logging over on this side was a lot rougher. It was. Yeah. The ground was rougher, steeper. There was some rough ground upriver, too, you know, at the tail end of their operation there. They had a lot of nice ground. Geez. Them camps all around Bovill was real nice ground. Two, four, five, six, seven. They was all nice. Well, just a little rolling. You know, little humps here and there. Well, you could lay a real nice deal any place putting there. Very little bridge. Oh, a nice big mine. So, they didn't, uh, that's probably why they didn't do so much movement and stuff like that. They didn't do anything. No, they laid the river right next to the stump. They had quite a big river crew working there. They all had them marrying steam-raised loads of tongs, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Art Henderson? No, I've heard of him, even though, well, who was he?

speaker: William Stowell
words: He was a... He loaded cars for years, then he got to be a push. He got killed up here at Camp Wye.

speaker: William Stowell
words: He was running that camp then. But he'd come up below a bulldozer, you know. Bulldozers were working above him. He'd come up from below and they were shoving over trees and snags and everything, you know, in one of them. He got underneath one of them. He was a good guy. Yeah, he was.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I think, uh... Oh, do you know, uh... Christ. I can't think of his name, but he married Art's daughter anyway. He's some kind of a... wheel over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Bovill.

speaker: William Stowell
words: They haven't got much of anything going over there, have they?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, they got... Well, they got some work there.

speaker: William Stowell
words: They run these four ... quick out here, aren't they?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know. They've been out there, I've noticed they're logging around there lately. They used to have a big cap right in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, right down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the hell?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, the old Camp 8 was there. That was years ago. That's before my time. Yeah, I've heard old Camp 8. I've heard of that quite a bit. But this other cap was built, the car cap was built, was set up right there where Camp 8 was.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, you think that country was more... It seemed to me, I had the idea that a lot of people, more people maybe, in the woods there, were people who had families and both of them. Yes, yes, they did. A lot of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make the camps more settled than on this side?

speaker: William Stowell
words: I don't know. This was always kind of a... when it first started up, it was kind of a hurly-burly outfit, you know. The old Tramp Lumberjacks, all of them working there.

speaker: William Stowell
words: There was no married men like the ... There was a lot of married men working in the ..., in the woods, in the camps, you know. Quite a few of them. Because you could tell on Sunday the camp was in right out, you know. And here, of course, they get gyppoed. All there, it says it's a week.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Tommy Kenny was superintendent, then he come over here from Montana and took over. There was several pushes come here from Montana. Tommy Stevens, Gabe Herman, Solveley McHenry, and Kenny, of course. Kenny was superintendent here until 36.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Then he went to the Wynton out. He was down in California for a while for his River Lumber Company. But he's dead now, Kenny is. Bradbury took over after him here, Howard. He was dead. He's dead. He died, he was 75 because he died on the ... Retired Unit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you first came out here in the 20s, was there much going on on this side in the way of lumber? What's most of it up around Bow Hill in that country?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh no, this over here, well this mill started here in 26, in August of 26.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were just getting going pretty big then?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, yeah. They hadn't got into the river yet. But they were railroad camps out of Pierce and JP and headquarters. Oh yeah, they put in a lot of timber here that 27 days, 100 cars a day. Then they got into the river, you see. They would cut a strip into the river and they built a road in a big island. Then they started up the river camp there. B, C, D, E, F, G.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that before the depression started again?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. Well the first drive was 28, so you can see they had to get in there. They logged in 27th Camp B and C. Yeah, B and C. Camp A, that was at, Camp A was right there where that dam is built now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right there near Orofino?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, well all they took out of there was piling for bridges and stuff for the mill, you know. That little mill, that's all that involved. There was no timber there much except for the bridge.

speaker: William Stowell
words: A lot of people never knew where that camp was. Camp A. Camp B.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what that first one was like they had on the river?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, sure I do. I remember all about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who the hell had that web?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Joshua Webb. I think he must be dead though, we have to. But they drove from Big Island then, you see. The river had big islands. Them B and C and them camps put that timber in, all flumed.

speaker: William Stowell
words: And then the next spring,

speaker: William Stowell
words: the next spring they drove from Beaver Creek. They had gotten into the river and started camps up there. And old Brooks brought that drive down as far as Big Island and Webb took it from there on in. But then the next year, old Brooks went all the way through. They had one again, as you see. Well, Webb had one again too, but Brooks had to build them too up on the way up the north part. But his were just kind of a skimpy affair compared to what old Webb had. Webb had regular, I guess you could cross the oaks now. They were all square-tempered, bolted together. But the ones old Brooks built, they were, there was no bolts or spikes or nothing in them, just all made out of woods, you know. They, there were different lengths of cedars. I guess a lot this way, you know. And they were real flexible. Stuff about, oh, 18-20 inches on the butt, it was. Poured in 60 feet long. They were, and they were held together by, they were about 85 feet long, possibly about 22 feet wide. I helped build them. They were held together by what they called widths. They were wild cherries. And they laid these binders across.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These what?

speaker: William Stowell
words: These binders, you know, they were mostly mined maple. And these holes, they were boards, you know, these, uh, embers. These, this here, that the raft was made out of, was boards, you see. And then they drew over, they put this width in and a square peg with it, you know. And around, oh, 5-8ths of the size. And that's what they were together. Just this width over this, uh, lined maple, this binder. You couldn't, you had to, if you wanted to dismantle them, you had to cut them up. You couldn't, there was nothing coming apart otherwise. So it was pretty tough. Oh, yeah, sure. But flexible, you know. In rough water they'd oscillate, you know, up and down. So there was no breakage in them that way. This was the first, the first drive. Yeah, this was the first one. Yeah, they used them all the time after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the same time?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Same time, right up to the last drive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, where'd it?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Except they put rubber on, they had rubber, you see, the last, uh, they had, oh, I don't know, 10 years maybe, they had, uh, pontoons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think, huh? This, the first drive, were they well, well constructed, well made?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it serviceable, did they do the job?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, yes, yes, truly they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the first drive go?

speaker: William Stowell
words: I mean, I take it they hadn't had it, they'd never drove them down to Clearwater before. No, no. It would have been a new experience for them. Well, uh, there was an old guy by the name of Piper he did years ago. He run the first rafts, and he knew the river, he lived on the river. He was an old, uh, prospector, but he'd racked a, a cedar poles on that river when they used to, well I call it piratin' or hygradin' or whatever the hell you want to call it, before this dam was built, you know, they'd make up wood drafts and cedar rafts and run them down into the snake and on down and sell them, you see. Well, he used to do that with his business, he done that. Well, he knew every pebble in that north part of the season, he run the first rafts down for the company. Helped build them, he built them, you know, he was a, a super right of the element in building them, so that's how they made it with that part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they hire him on just for his job? Was he working for that before, or was he an independent gippo?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, he was just independent. He used to make up a cedar pole, he did that, he did it, you know. That's before the company started writing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this pirate, this piratin', was this really company timber and man was taking it?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, anybody on the bank of the river, you know, standing close to the bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anybody?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, they done a lot of it, yeah. Everybody was doing it. Wood and cedar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did I make a living that way?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, for, cedar you put, yeah. Did you have to follow, did you have to come down raft, you had to come down on your raft, make sure it made it. Yeah, oh yeah. I wonder where they sold, where they sold, how far down they had to go. Oh, I don't know. A lot of them were sold and made up in the post, cedar posts, you know. They're at a premium down river, you know. There's no timber down there, down that snake, you know what that is. Oh yeah. Probably wouldn't have to go too far once you get back in it. Tumblebees all day.

speaker: William Stowell
words: But, yeah, they made a living at it. This old piper was a fine old guy. They found him dead in his cabin. That's where he drove here, that's where he was a funny old guy. He'd never come to town. He'd get out here, he'd land out here at the mill with them rafts and that is when you're driving by the company, you know. And hitchhike back up river. He was a regular old pig, you know. I don't think he ever went to a barber or anything like that. He was just an old hermit, that's what he was. But, a good old guy, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever talk to him and know how long he'd been in this country?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, he'd been here for years. Yeah, he built, I don't know how many cabins he built on that north part. He's trapped, you know. He'd get, well he'd wander off, you know, go and look in his trap line over and he'd have a cabin to stay in. And he built, you know, just lean-to cabins of all time up and down that north side. There was another guy come on after, Tyler Joe, Cuckie his name. He built a half a dozen cabins too, but he built a couple of them. He made a living, and he'd work on the driver in the spring. He was a good driver, very French Canadian. Real good. He died up there right next to the guy in the cab.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's the Tyler Joe come from? He didn't talk?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, he wasn't talking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At all?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, he'd say a word or two of course, but he wouldn't try to keep the conversation going. He'd just sit there. I used to come up and see him, you know, after he had quit working. He was too old to work. And we'd go in, I used to go in a little earlier than the driving crew, you know, they'd be five or six of us and we'd go in and build him racks, you know. And, oh, he lived about a mile from where we'd be building him, and I'd go up to see him. He'd sit there, he wouldn't talk. I'd keep on talking, you know, and he'd ask to know is all he'd say. There was nothing in the shack, you know, an old fine van and a couple of old kettles of some kind. So, wooden bunk, I said, well, I'm going to go, Joe. Oh, sit down, sit down. So I'd have to sit another half hour. He was everything. I'd finally get out of there. But he died right there. He never come out. Same thing as Gus Piper. He trapped and he'd done the river drives too. Gus Piper.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. He didn't drive too long. He was down there about five years. He was back up there one spring and they found him dead in the doorway of his shack. They don't know whether it was fair beads or foul or what happened. I don't think it could be foul because there's nobody ever around there. I think he just died. He was pretty well decomposed when they found him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This Piper?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. Gus Piper. Let's see who took them Rafts then. Frank Larkins. He's dead too, though. He run them then. He wasn't. He was good as all does. And then they had different ones after that. I used to work on them all the time. Helped move them, you know. But I never did run them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When that first year, how many of them did they have?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Rafts. They had one, two.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Three. They had a sleeping raft, a cook raft, and a chuck raft. You see, they couldn't get no chuck in. There was no roads in there then. Until they got to Big Island. See, there was, I don't know, 60 miles there, I guess. And the rear was heavy and you were moving slow, you know. That is, a mile a day or something like that, you know. It took a lot of chuck. That's why they had the chuck raft. They had a regular crew that, well, there was one man that run the rafts. Then they'd pick up, he'd pick up, bend out off the crew, you see, to help it move. And they usually got a day off that. Well, it took a day. There was a lot of hiking. You see, say you moved five miles, you had to hike 15 miles back three different times.

speaker: William Stowell
words: And then if you missed a landing or something, which we did. That caused more complications then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you miss a landing? Had they roped it back or something?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh no, oh no, you're impossible to do that. I think that was the question. No, you went to the next one. Or you got in someplace where you thought you could. This old piper knew every bend in the river. And so did Frank Larkin. They were lucky that way. They always had someone that knew.

speaker: William Stowell
words: But later on when they got motors on them, they put motors on them in rubber rafts. That was the first one they put the motors on.

speaker: William Stowell
words: They didn't draw that much water from the pontoon. If you go up river and place it with them, you could come to the dead and stop any place. That is not any place, but if the water wasn't too wicked, that's a mighty hall. Yeah, that simplified everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men would run that raft in the old days? How many men would move it?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, they'd be usually five.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They used poles?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, sweeps. Long oars, no. There was an 18 foot blade on about a 40 foot stem. The bow board usually had three men. You'd run back and forth across with it. The guy would run from the stern, the pilot. He'd call the shots from the stern. If he wanted to go over to the right, he'd holler right. And then you'd always position anyhow, you see. Then you'd give your oar and give her a gut to the right. The oar would be about this high.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Above your head there?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. That is the back end of it, you know. The shorter man would be down there. He'd go to the edge. Yeah, he'd be reaching up. But you could bring up the time. You'd take quite a few clouds sometimes, you know. Landing was about the worst part of it. They'd run through an ordinary eddy, you know. They were so heavy. If you went in the upper end of the eddy, they'd either swing on you or run through. If it wasn't a real strong eddy, they'd swing on you. They'd load up with water in front. You know, water would be up here. And they'd swing. Then there you were. You would be held. There used to be trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you do?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, try to straighten it out. You couldn't run from the stern because the stern was loaded. Way down the stern was in the water, you know. They'd water over the stern, you see. It was too heavy to handle. The bow was up. You had to work it from the bow. You'd turn again if you could to get the bow ahead and then try to land. If you got them, well, you had two rafts, say, a sleeping raft and a cook raft and a chuck raft. Well, if you got them, you had to have them together, you know. At night, when the crew come in, if you didn't have them, they couldn't be a mile apart, you know. If you missed one, you'd have to pack it up with the other one and go where the first one was. Then maybe you'd have to hike three or four miles back to the rear in the morning. That was no good. They used to try to keep them as close to the rear as possible, you know. That was the idea. Eliminate that hiking, you know, because when you're marching, you ain't fighting, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what kind of a spot did a pilot have to pick out on the river? Would it have to be a real sizable cove or something like that?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, that would be the best. And the bank, you know, they used to try to get a place. He wouldn't run into a bus pilot or nothing. You know, you had wash sinks and stuff to set out on the bank. And build fires for the crew at night. And they had places, you know, after the first couple of years, they had places, regular places where they landed at the roof. If the river was moving past, they'd miss one or two of them, you see, otherwise they'd run in there and tie up, you know. Well, we used to have snub lines. And we had an old boat, an old bass hole. We'd go ahead and set this snub line, you see. It was, oh, just a long, it was two inch lines, I think, with a bowl in it, you know. And you'd set that and put a stick or something on it so you could see it, you know. So the guy had to bail off of that raft because it would be tooting right along, you see. And you had a shorter line on the raft, maybe 30 feet, with a big snap in it, you see. A big iron snap that long with a spring in it, you know. Well, you'd grab that and bail off, you know, the guy that was supposed to do the snub. And there was always one good active man down there, you know. And you'd grab this line with the bowl in it and there was a raft on the tree, you see, one raft, it was a little, a tree or something. Well, you'd make the joint then, you see, with the big snap in the bowl. And then you'd drop that and grab the other line, you see, and pay out a little slack. If you snubbed it up real tight and it was in straight water, you know, it would take the stern of the raft down and probably break the line at that point, too. But they could just give a little slack, you know, on the stump and you could snub it anytime you wanted to as far as you had the hitch here to do it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But that spring cushioned the spring?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, no, that spring, that spring was just to keep the lines together. It wasn't a coil spring, it was just a spring that kept the lines. After it'd gotten the snap, you know, it couldn't fly up. No, there was no coil spring. Oh, it sounds like you didn't have, you had no time to play. Oh, no, no, no. That'll be done. If you missed, you was looking at them going around the bend and there you were, you didn't know where the hell they were going. And you made a kind of a nape out of yourself, too. That was part of it. They'd kid you about that, you know. And maybe find someone else to do it, if you missed too often.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Now the deal on this was that they would take one raft at a time down, and then go back to the next one. But when they got the rubber, you know, that's what we asked just a few years they had that rubber. But that was all in one. That was 118 feet long. Motor on each end. It was in three sections. Now, they was awful flexible, them things. Rough to ride, you know. Rough water just like a bunco jumping around. But when you landed at that thing, everything was there. You didn't have to go back after nothing. I'll bet the old timers must have really thought that was quite a change. Oh, yeah. The new fella on the drive never could imagine what them old days were like. No. He's... No, I remember one time we missed... We missed with the Slickman Wanigan at Robeson Island. That's just above the Little North Fork. And we never got tied up until we got to Big Island. I don't know what in hell the idea was. It was 30 miles there.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I was new on that too then. I didn't know where the hell it was going. But the pilot did. Larkent, he knew. But I don't know what the hell was there. Nobody ever did find out. He got tanned anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For that?

speaker: William Stowell
words: For that. Well, he did about... When we got to Big Island, there was another raftman there. But I hiked back up. I don't know. We had sleep on the bank then. Well, we was 12 days on that bar and we slept on the bank. We had the cook raft, you see. We landed the cook raft. Another crew moved that. They landed no trouble, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It took 12 days to get the sleeping raft?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah. Sleep on the bank every night. And nobody gets much. They built a big fire all along to move that house. Two or three men couldn't move all day. Keep your feet from ever snowing around up there. Howard Bradbury was keeping time that spring. Yeah, we can search too. That was the year Roosevelt was elected, I think. Yeah, that was in the spring. That was just the start of the spring. Well, that's a hell of a thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that guy, was something wrong with him or something?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, I never could figure it out. Nobody ever did. Everybody tried to. He missed the landing alright and after that he didn't, oh he had probably a couple of other times, but he didn't make much. I knew myself he was going to miss some other ones. The line broke, you see, is what happened at the Roberts and the First Manchins. He broke the snub line, you know, and lost a man off the raft, you see. The guy that went short, he couldn't buy a raft here. He was there. But they broke the line and, oh, that didn't make any difference. He could have got in someplace. The water wasn't too high. It was up pretty well, but not, hadn't had the big water yet. It was the big water that came after that. I never could figure out why. But he got relieved of his command when soon that old post caught up to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Without the snub line, how would you stop it?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, you'd have to improvise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With the war line?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh yes. It could pretty much rig up anything.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, there's places where you wouldn't need one. You could step off, you know. Or either you had to gell up while you could get around something, you know, and get stopped.

speaker: William Stowell
words: You were new, so you didn't know what was going on too good. We were all new. Oh, I see. You guys were waiting for your orders. Yeah. Yeah. There was no Rasputin on there, except, well, Bill Aiken. And Larkin, he was supposed to be the big shot. But I don't know, he never made it as successive as I could see. But that's the way it was. But after that day, well...

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh yeah, Bill Aiken took them then. He run them for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long do you remember how long that first drive took?

speaker: William Stowell
words: By gosh, no. You see, it was split up, you know. It changed pushes, those big islands, you see. Another push went down from there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean the first drive?

speaker: William Stowell
words: The very first drive. Oh, I don't remember, no. Cast away for the summer. No machinery or nothing, you know. Everything, the water got away and everything was, the river was new to them. They got late water that year in June, but it didn't, it helped, of course, but it dropped right away again. We were down there on them big bars in the main river, cranking them logs off there by hand. Hot. That's not very interesting.

speaker: William Stowell
words: What, what, uh... You know, these log drives, I never did see one myself. And up north there, of course, we never had any, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the men working in the water on the logs, what did, uh, what was the techniques that they used? What did they do to keep them logs moving? What did they have to do?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, take like the river crew, they, uh, they gang up around the logs and roll it in the water, roll it in the river. That's all, that's all they could do. Just keep rolling, it just went afloat. Then there was another crew, more experienced, and, uh, well, they weren't young men or they weren't old men. They were, been around 35, you know, they were in the center for, they would take the floating stuff, you know. You know, like, uh, you land on the center, you know, the center's in the center of the river, you know. And it's held there by something, you know, rock or something, you know, and they pile up, they get to be real long, you know. But you make space in the front, get slack in front, you see, that's the first thing you gotta do. Oh, it all depends on the water and, uh, general conditions, you know, you get slack and, and then it'll start to peel off, you see. Well, sometimes it'll go, sometimes it'll hang again and pile up more. That makes it bad then. You've got to, when it, uh, starts to go, you hang on to it with the boat as long as you can, you see. Unless there's a shot or something in there, then you've got to get the hell away from it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A what?

speaker: William Stowell
words: A shot, you know, powder. Sometimes you use powder, you see. But with these motorboats you could always come back. But, uh, that's always, you couldn't, you could have to line them back. That took, uh, well, different length of time, of course, sometimes that was an awful job, depends on how fast the water was and how far downriver you went. And you had to line it back, the crew would line it back up, up way above the center so you could land on it again, you know. That's why they didn't use so much powder then, because you had to get away from it when you put a shot in, you know. And they didn't want to leave it, you know, they wanted to stay and pick on it until, uh, until it unrivaled, you see. If they were sure it was going to go, they'd leave it. And, uh, oftentimes they did. Most of the time they, after you got slack, they'd start picking on them a while and they'd peel off, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say line them back up, would you explain that? What do you mean?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, they all got a, what they call a painter on them, you know, a rope in the bow. And, uh, oh, it's probably, it's a small rope, that's why they, maybe. Well, the boatmen stay in and they pull, keep it offshore, and the rest of the crew will yell and get a hold of that line on the bank and crawl along the bank and pull the goddamn thing back up. You see, that's, nobody likes to do that. That's why they hang out with them, send them so long, you see, they'd stay right on there, you know. They'd land in a place with a back pretty well from the front, you know, getting amongst, between the logs, you see, so they could stay there until it went. Then they'd go.

speaker: William Stowell
words: But I don't know, I'd go there three days and a half on spring, that's as long as it took. And I've driven there a hundred days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's, uh, how do you get, how could you do it in three and a half days, what would that condition?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, the river's at Elk Creek, you know, that is. Yes, sir. Well, that's where the river was, and, I don't know, that was in, forty-eight. And we stayed in town, we only had one boat, there was only four or five, six of us, maybe six I think there was. But the water come up, but Lord God, you couldn't, you couldn't keep up to the river. We had dinner at a shaka that day, it was from Elk Creek, you can figure that out. Then out into the main river we went, oh, well, it was on the highway, you know. That's Kerry Lane, and the water was right in the highway. Webb was the pushback here, Young Webb, Bill Webb, that's old, Joplin, some, he was coming from town every year. And the next, on a Sunday we finished up, we were at, I don't know, I think we left it in a lap away or something, maybe it was nothing anyway anymore, three days in a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is he still alive?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yes, he was a push on that driver, good push, he lives,

speaker: William Stowell
words: third, I'm third someplace, I know where he lives but I can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old is he? He retired now?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, he retired, he was pulling around the pond out here for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was, well now, you gonna be around tomorrow?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, I can't, I can't tell you tomorrow. I won't be around anymore. This lady that attended by then here, she lived two houses for profit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where, at the Silver Dollar?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, in the morning, she's there in the morning, not tomorrow morning, but she's there on Tuesday, Wednesday. Well, I'm back here another time, I'll find out. I'm here, huh. I haven't seen profit but once since he left the woods, he runs camp too, you know. Yeah, he's a real guy. I didn't think he was that old, I drove with him on the Port of the River. Oh Christ, I thought I was just as young as I was then. He was too, he isn't as old as I am. He's about 66, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was he born?

speaker: William Stowell
words: He was 93.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was 72, right?

speaker: William Stowell
words: April.

speaker: William Stowell
words: How much of work, when these guys were on the river working on the drive, were they on the logs standing right on the... Oh, well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a rare thing or was that pretty often you did, you done that?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Depends on what you were doing, if you were cleaning a netty or something like that, you'd be working on timber all the time. But the rear crew, they weren't on the logs unless they wanted to be, you know. If anyone wanted to know, they'd get around and get in the slough or something and get pulled around. But no, there wasn't too much work on loose timber except them centers. Picking a jam once in a while, you'd get on the stuff, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of the time they worked from the bateau though?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, they'd be out of the bateau, of course. It wouldn't be in the bateau, they'd be out working on the timber. But the bateau would be there to pick them up in case they went adrift or got hurt or got dinged up somewhere, you know, where there was any way and capacity to pick them up. Unless they got scattered, that's one thing a boat man didn't like, if they got scattered on, you know, too much, you see. Like a center breaking up, you know, and the crew gets scattered and they couldn't pick them all up. Maybe someone wasn't capable of handling themselves in that position, you know, just get excited and get out of hand. They didn't like, but mostly they got along pretty good. They lost about four men on their river. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, did you ever see anybody go, get lost?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, yes, I see them three years a little over that spring. We would pick them up, but we were too late. We were just above them. They were on the right-hand side of the river going down just above the bridge. There was trees there, you know, and there was half a dozen bullpunt trees scattered around there. The water was maybe six feet deep, but all was strong, you know. It was smooth, but running fast, you know, strong water. You couldn't swim against it or nothing like that, you know. You couldn't blow a boat against it, you know. Well, there was a boat working just below us on this one of these trees, you know, taking this timber off the trees. And when the center, when it moved out, you see, when they got slack and everything, of course, it just picked up and left and there was a big sweeper, big steer. Oh, wife, pulling the stump, full of tree length, stumping everything, you know. Underneath that, you couldn't see it, and it come up under the boat, you know, and swamped the boat. They come up on the bow and the stern went down, you know, and it just rolled right around underneath.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, they was, these guys that ground her were in the boat. But there was four of them up the tree, went up this tree.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, of course, they, I seen it, I was looking at it. Oh, I was near that house, I guess, up river in the other boat. We were down in the center there. Some of the time they were going to swamp that boat, they'd seen it coming back, you know. So we all jumped in, the boatman hollers first, jumped in. There was only four of us, we had four boats. And we took after them, you see. We left the guys in the tree, we didn't stop for them at all. We could see one guy, he had his head just going under the bridge as we got closer. We couldn't get to him and he disappeared. The water was high and awful dirty, you know. You couldn't see nothing. So, I don't know, we landed, I guess, at Cottonwood Island. There was some guy going down along the bank. He was in the water, too. I think that was, the name was Chase. There was Fiddy, Chase, and Anderson. They were the names of the guys that found us. And, Miss Anderson, her, his daughter works in the liquor store, that little farm. She's 42 years old now. But anyhow, we landed at Cottonwood Island on the highway side, and it was on a Sunday, and, oh, got a bit of traffic. But I got up there and stopped an old flatbed truck coming along and we put the boat on it. It was way longer than the old flatbed, but three or four of us got in the bow and held it down and tied it on. We had to come back up and land, put that boat in the water, and land on that tree. Where them guys were, that was a tricky proposition. But we made her. Tomorrow.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Or, if Ben Tom was one of them, he lives in Orofino now.

speaker: William Stowell
words: He runs a, no he don't run nothing now, he works for construction, he's a 63, I think, now. But he was one of the guys up the tree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what year was that?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Because me and this gal over here in the liquor store, you know, I get whiskey for the silver dollar, you know. I got a little venture over there, but then, but anyhow, I used to talk to her, you know, nice gal. Said, you knew my dad, that's my first time getting whiskey there three or four years ago. I said, yes, sure. But I didn't know who she was talking about, I was shooting in the dark, you have to wait till she volunteered some information, which she did right away, you know, she said. Walt Anderson started. I knew Walt very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So these three guys never had a chance, really?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh no, not after they got in the water, they didn't have no chance, no. No, no. Unless, the only chance they had was they stayed with the boat. The boat didn't really swamp until it hit the pier, you know. It hit the bridge pier. And then it went under. We found that down, around Cottonwood Creek. Washed up on a bar, but it was not serviceable anymore, you know. But, I don't know, there was spare pedals in that boat, and everything underneath the seats. I tied them in there myself. When we started out, you know, I had two pedals tied underneath, there was nothing away or nothing. Because the gravel was one of them, you know, even the boat, them boats, they're, well, they'll be just about ready to sink and they'll still pack three or four men, you know. But they could have done something if they'd have stayed with that boat, they could have stayed off of that pier, wrapped them pedals. Of course, always and everything was gone, but, oh, I don't know, they'd have shied it, I guess. Yeah, in a situation like that, you couldn't really do it. They found them all but one. You know, the Valtaise and Anderson, but they never found fitting. His brother, this guy that drowned, his name was Ray. I can't think of the brother's name, but I know him real well. He was on the Eastbound Police Force here. And he was a Chief of Detectives in Cheyenne at the time this happened. And he came up, he and his wife, and he stayed here all summer. He went to work on the police force here then. And every Sunday they'd take a boat and go up to Lenoir and put the boat in the river and she'd drive the car down and he'd look every Sunday all summer long and it never comes. I suppose he got sanded in, you know, someplace, buried you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When that kind of thing happened, death or bad accident, is there any protection for families or relatives?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William Stowell
words: You mean the insurance?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, from the company. Well, I don't know about the company. The state I know pays. Them guys got around $8 ,000 I think is all, but we took up collections in the camp for them. We got damn near that much in the camp. After the drive was over, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: $8 ,000 for the three of them?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, but they got $8 ,000 apiece out of the state. That would, you know, they kind of hung off on the fitting for, maybe they haven't paid it yet, but they claim seven years they got it paid. So I suppose that's paid now. That's pretty good to them given that much money. That sounds like a lot of money. Oh, yeah, but them guys are all pretty easy, you know, in case of that kind. They got about that much for the three of them, you know. It used to be customary to have a collection taken up on a drive, you'd give a day's wages. That was whatever the wage was. But of course I never paid that all. I'd hit her a little heavier, you know. I'd spend her anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? A collection on a drive if somebody was killed?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yes, or hurt, or sick or something, and take up the collections. You know, it used to be way more so than, they don't take up many collections in these camps anymore, you know. But I was a young lad around here, every day there was somebody to take up the collections. Well, if you'd given everyone those, you wouldn't have no salary left. You'd give them a day that worked every day. No, no, but I mean, it wasn't every day, you know, but they'd be every couple of months, they'd be somewhere around. But they were all worthy of it, too. You know, up north there, it seemed like whenever the lumbering shut down, the men always spoke hands. Oh, yes. Yes, yes, yes. Until the Clearwater, when they started up piers for the town. Some people never, some guys never got by piers for years. I don't think they come out the other way by elsewhere. But I was in piers, I don't know, I must have been there twenty years anyway. You know, whenever the piers, everything there you wanted.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I forget, I didn't know there was any small town, you know, that had that. I thought you had to go to a big city. The piers always had them, they haven't now, but there was always something like that there. Yeah. They, I don't think there's any there now. Was there much, there's a lot of lodging then, too. Lots of places where the jack to stay. The houses I'm heading will push you back. I was there when I was there.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Uh, yeah. Well, you know where the Clearwater Hotel is. There was a big tent there. When I was the first man up there, there was, I don't know how many men, the railroad was up at JP then, the fire JP. It wasn't in the headquarters yet. But, I don't know, there must have been three thousand men around there. I don't know how many, but it was coming and going. And they had all kinds of camps along the railroad, and railroad camps, and campers and all that. But that's where it was when I went up there at JP. This thing along was truly the headquarters in Maryland. But that old Clearwater Hotel was a big tent, and it was in the army cots. You know, three or four floors in the bottom, just on the ground. I don't know what they started, a dollar I guess.

speaker: William Stowell
words: I forget who built that. Oh, it was Charlie and Chris Johnson. I think that's Clearwater Hotel. Then old Snider, he built the piercings. I was asked about that too. He built just one old shack that he was pooped right in there, and he built all around it. He made a hotel. It's still there. No, it's burnt down. The bank, there's a bank there. I got it. We were maybe one of them. Oh yeah. It was always about three. Old back yard was one in the last year. So it got down to two, and then one. The piercings are very old. That is actually the case, and it's the one that is the first. The old.

speaker: William Stowell
words: It was never any there, was it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it was, but they were gone by 1915. They were gone by 1915, something like that. I think Clarkia stayed pretty wide open. Clarkia was held in the bank. Everything. Save his piercings. There was a big dog. Oh no, oh no. Well, they used to go in that way, you know. I'm not dry.

speaker: William Stowell
words: They used to go in, Clarkia. You'd think they were coming out that way, but they were the way they performed, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're talking about marble Christians?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, but they never come out that way. See the marble craters were run into St. Joe at Bullough Avery. Marble Creek Station was the next one in the road.

speaker: William Stowell
words: You'd think they were coming out that way, but the bootleggers treated them. They gave them everything they wanted. Gals, drugs, of course. And the girls were very free with their wares too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they doing on the marble creek? How was the logging on the marble creek? What kind of things did they have going there? Didn't they have inclines?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, they had inclines coming down to the main line of Milwaukee. Yeah, that would give or do that thing. I know what you were saying. It's pretty bad. That was double track lay out. The empties come up and the loads went down. No, it wasn't double track. It had an automatic switch. That was it. I rode up on it. It had a donkey sitting on the top, you know. A big donkey, I don't know, it was a big vlammet, 12 or 15, anyway. And they used to drop them cars down. It must have been steep. It was 57 % in one place.

speaker: William Stowell
words: That's just exactly what it was because I asked, oh, what's his name?

speaker: William Stowell
words: First time I went in there. I asked him how steep that steep place was. He said 57 % and I thought he was lying to me, you know. That was Bill Gaffney. He was the superintendent then. I thought he was lying to me. I didn't say anything, but I asked several others. It was 57%. I guess it was. Did they live in there too before they had the... They used chutes. Yeah, that's right. They shot under the railroads.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there some kind of a fire or something there?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A fire?

speaker: William Stowell
words: There's some kind of a... I can't remember what I heard. I heard something about... Well, it was a fire there in 1910, that's all. Oh, I heard about that one, yeah. I have a whole state of Idaho front and end. They didn't try to put it out, yes. I know they didn't. They couldn't. They had no way to get to it. But, no, I don't know when you fired it or lately. Yeah.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Well, that was... See, that was the Rutledge Timber Company, yeah.

speaker: William Stowell
words: And then they... I thought they took it over. They kind of... They didn't do much thereafter. They didn't buy gosh, they trucked there now. They went right in Clarkia, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I worked up there, they was several different creeks, hobo creeks, you know, these creeks?

speaker: William Stowell
words: I don't know how many... I worked there in hobo. But I got it... Wood Em Up George, they called him. I've heard that name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You have, eh?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Sure. Whoa.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Oh, boy. There was never another one like him. He was a... He was a dibble, that fella. He got killed down in Oregon. I knew him real well. I knew the whole family. He had two boys and a girl. And his wife. Yeah, he... he was camped for... He was dibbled for himself on marble. That's when I worked first, caught up to him. But he went broke there. He had pretty fair outfits, 14 teams of horses. All the rigging to go with him, you know. Well, eh, then he went to work for the Diamonds. He worked there for years around the camp. But I guess they didn't like his style, something that they dumped him in anyway. But he had quite a bit of money, you know, and all the little dandy home in Spokane. And he lost everything. He went to the racehorse, you know. He was a forest salute. He was making money, handled a fish. But he bought three old clubs and he started running them around, following them around Seattle, Portland, Tacoma, and Spokane. Of course, they knew he was on the lot. They had to go back to working the woods. He couldn't work, you know, himself. He was too old to work. Everything had changed. He never changed with conditions, you know, at all. It never did, you know. He, eh, I know when the dozers first come out, the Diamonds bought dozers, you know. He wouldn't use it. He skipped him a hundred miles instead of building a truckload with a dozer and the dozer sitting in the camp yard. That didn't pay him. I think that's one reason why he... This, uh, Wood-em-up, I heard he got that name because he was always trying to get one more log on the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah, that's it.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Wood-em-up. And he was, uh, I don't know, he liked to know the right idea. He was an awful man to firemen, you know. I seen him fire thirty men in one morning. Thirty men, that's it. He had them, there was them, uh, Labor-Aids and ... in Van Camp and Featherstone and, oh, I don't know how they formed it. He kept them busy.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he, uh, what did he fire him for? No reason?

speaker: William Stowell
words: No, no, no. He fired him back the next day when he seen him. Oh, he just gets mad. He started camping. He can't one, he might have a reason for canning the first one, but if you happened to come along after that, he was gone too. I worked for him five months one time and never got dead. I never did get canned. He never did can me. I don't know why. I used to always, if I thought he would come, I'd quit, you see. I'd kind of reach between the lines a little bit.

speaker: William Stowell
words: He was a good guy. But he was a good guy. You could always go along and stay with the light in his camp. He might commit that he has nothing to do with his age. He'd stick around for a day or two. The rest of the time, I guess. Yeah, he had a lot of good times.

speaker: William Stowell
words: One of his boys is a contractor now. Yano and company, his name was Yano. Y-A-R-E-N-O. But he was a construction contractor. He built them sewers in the week, I know that. I never, I haven't seen him since. You know, talking about this guy's, you know, staying overnight and finding out if there's work. I've heard one guy be telling me about these camp inspectors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there many guys that went around the camps and tried them all out?

speaker: William Stowell
words: Sure, that, well, you see around Bovill, I've met thirty men between Camp 7 and Bovill on the railroad track.

speaker: William Stowell
words: They never said nothing. They'd have to set up extra tables for them. I never did go that route. I'd go to a camp and get over the main when I was looking for work. That was customers. They'd go in the camp and if the puss wanted you, he'd come around and tell you. And if he didn't, well, he'd come and tell you anything. I said, alright, then you could go. Or go to work. I usually went to work. I don't know, I was pretty lucky. I knew a lot of them. And a lot of the crew, you see, the crew would plug in for you once in a while.

speaker: William Stowell
words: Like over on the Bovill side, there was Mike Sturgell, I guess he's living it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oscar Sturgell?

speaker: William Stowell
words: He died recently. Moscow. He'd been dead just this year. He always had the best horses. And if a guy come along, there'd be some guy in the crew, of course, maybe you might know half of the crew.
